description: World-specific biome descriptor overlays for Threadhallow. Applied on top of shared biome_descriptors.json to add thread/weave metaphors, Loom Stone references, and ward-circle atmosphere to the LLM context. A forest in Threadhallow should feel different from a forest in any other world.

terrain: threadline shimmer between the trunks
ward-stones marking the tree line
paths worn by Threadwalker patrols
sounds: the low hum of a nearby Threadline
a Binding Song fragment drifting from a distant village
the creak of a tree growing against its pattern
smells: burnt lavender from an old ward-mark
copper tang where a Threadline runs close to the ground
atmosphere: the feeling of being inside a woven thing
a sense that the trees were placed, not grown
watchfulness that isn't animal — it's structural

terrain: Threadlines visible as heat-shimmer ribbons on clear days
Loom Stone pillars on distant hilltops
grass growing in weft-and-warp patterns after rain
sounds: wind carrying the hum of the Bright Weave in summer
a faint drone from the nearest ward-circle
the snap of a Threadline adjusting — like a bowstring plucked by nothing
smells: clean ozone where the weave is healthy
the old-wool scent that follows thin places
atmosphere: exposure — nothing between you and the pattern of the sky
the uneasy knowledge that the nearest ward is hours away

terrain: Loom Stones at summit cairns, carved with Weaver's Marks
Threadlines visible along ridgelines like luminous veins
stone split by ancient weave stress — cracks filled with a faint crystalline residue
sounds: the Threadlines singing in the wind — a harmonic that changes with elevation
Weaver's echoes in narrow canyons — sounds that were never made repeating softly
smells: ozone at altitude where Threadlines converge
a mineral smell from Loom Stone dust
atmosphere: proximity to the Loom — the weave feels thinner in the vertical sense, as if reality has less padding at height
the sense that the mountains are knots in the Tapestry, load-bearing and permanent

terrain: ward-posts half-sunk and abandoned
Thread-residue in the water — faintly luminous scum on still pools
root systems that follow Threadline paths, forming unnatural straight lines
sounds: the gurgle of Fray-tainted water — a sound just off from normal
ward-chimes from a drowned settlement, still ringing below the waterline
smells: the wet-wool smell of thin places, permanent here
copper and rot where the weave has frayed into the water table
atmosphere: a place where the ward failed and the land remembers
the feeling of standing in someone's lost protection

terrain: standing stones that are actually ancient Loom Stone fragments
heather growing in spiral patterns around weave-active spots
cairns topped with Threadwalker offerings — woven tokens weathered to gray thread
sounds: the teeth-resonance hum of standing stones at dawn and dusk
wind that carries syllables — not words, just the shape of old speech
smells: peat smoke from hidden crofts inside makeshift ward-circles
the sharp green smell of heather flowering near a healthy Threadline
atmosphere: an old place — the hills were hills before the Weavers, and the weave lies thin over ancient bones
openness that feels like vulnerability without ward walls

terrain: ward-circle boundary stones at field edges, carved with binding-knots
crop rows that subtly follow the direction of underground Threadlines
scarecrows — some with fresh thread-knots at the wrists, a folk ward tradition
sounds: the low hum of the village ward-circle, constant as breathing
children's songs that are actually simplified Binding Song fragments
the creak of a warding-pole in the wind
smells: burnt lavender and copper at the ward boundary
clean earth inside the ward — the soil smells different where the weave is maintained
bread and woodsmoke from the settlement center
atmosphere: safety — the ward is close, the ward is working
the comforting lie of normalcy, three miles from a thin place

terrain: sea-stack Loom Stones visible at low tide, carved with salt-worn Weaver's Marks
Threadlines running out to sea — visible at dawn as light catches the weave over water
driftwood tangled with luminous thread-residue after storms
sounds: the Threadline hum blending with wave rhythm — the sea sings in a different key than land
ward-bells on fishing boats, ringing in patterns that are half-prayer, half-navigation
smells: salt and ozone together — the coast is where the weave meets something older
kelp that smells faintly of copper after a Fray-tide
atmosphere: the edge of the known pattern — the sea was not woven the same way as land
a frontier feeling, even in settled ports

terrain: Loom Stone veins in the rock walls — natural Threadline conduits, glowing faintly
crystal formations that grew along weave-stress lines
the carved marks of Weavers who worked underground — older than anything on the surface
sounds: the deep resonance of Threadlines in enclosed stone — a sub-bass felt in the bones
dripping water that falls in rhythms matching the ward-cycle
silence that is the absence of the surface hum, noticed only by its lack
smells: mineral dust with a copper edge
the dry-thread smell of ancient binding work
ozone near crystal formations
atmosphere: closeness to the Loom itself — underground, the metaphysical framework feels nearer
the unsettling knowledge that this is where the Weavers went deepest

terrain: trees woven into each other — roots and branches indistinguishable, as if the Weavers treated them as thread
Threadlines so dense they're visible as a permanent luminous haze
Weaver's Marks grown into living wood over a thousand years
sounds: the forest breathing — a collective exhalation of a thousand interlocked trees, rhythmic and slow
Threadline harmonics layered so thick they form a chord
whispers that might be Weaver-speech degraded by twelve hundred years
smells: the concentrated scent of living weave — resin, copper, ozone, all layered
old growth that smells of time itself
atmosphere: the densest weave outside of a Loom Stone — this is where the Tapestry was woven thickest
being inside a living thing — not nature, not building, but *pattern*

terrain: Threadlines frozen into the permafrost — visible as luminous veins under thin ice
Loom Stones half-buried by glacial drift, their marks pointing to constellations that have shifted
pine forests growing in geometric patterns that betray an underlying weave grid
sounds: the brittle hum of frozen Threadlines — a high, thin sound like a glass harmonica
ice cracking in sequences that map to Binding Song notation
smells: cold that smells of nothing — the weave preserves by freezing
pine resin sharper than normal, as if the cold concentrates everything
atmosphere: preservation — the cold has kept the weave intact here longer than anywhere
the ancient feeling of a pattern that hasn't been maintained because it hasn't needed to be

terrain: Loom Stones exposed by retreating snowline — some never visited by mortal feet
aurora-Threadlines that touch the ground in winter, leaving scorch-frost patterns in the snow
cairns built by Threadwalkers who came, marked, and never returned
sounds: the aurora hum — Threadlines singing overhead, too high and far for the ear but not for the chest
wind that carries the smell of the Fray from the far north, where nobody goes
smells: ozone from aurora-Threadlines
the flat nothing-smell of pure cold
occasionally: wet wool on the wind, the Fray's calling card
atmosphere: the world's edge — the Tapestry was woven thin here on purpose, or not woven at all
the knowledge that beyond the last Loom Stone, the Fray begins

terrain: sand-scoured Loom Stones with Weaver's Marks worn smooth — the oldest stones in the world, perhaps
Threadlines buried under sand, visible only as ripple patterns at certain sun angles
glass formations where Threadline energy and sand fused in ancient Fray events
sounds: the sub-harmonic of deep desert Threadlines — felt as vibration in the gut, not heard
sand singing over buried Loom Stones at certain wind speeds
smells: heated ozone where Threadlines surface through sand
dry copper — the weave's scent stripped of moisture
atmosphere: a place where the weave was woven tight and then dried — preserved but brittle
the paradox of an empty landscape that is structurally dense with buried pattern
